review.note: AI-drafted first pass - SME review required before production

sources.title: Smallpox Epidemic Strikes at the Cape (1713)
Three cases of bubonic plague are reported in Cape Town (1901)
Dr Chris Barnard performs the world's first human heart transplant
Hamilton Naki, laboratory assistant to cardiac surgeon Christiaan Barnard, dies

slides.title: Medicine Through Time
slides.lead: How people have cared for the sick - from traditional healers and healing plants to vaccines, antibiotics and heart transplants.

media.alt: A leafy healing plant on the left and a modern medical cross on the right, joined by an arrow showing change over time.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><g fill='#2f5233'><path d='M70 140 q-30 -30 -6 -60 q10 20 6 60 Z'/><path d='M70 140 q30 -30 6 -60 q-10 20 -6 60 Z'/><rect x='66' y='120' width='8' height='34'/></g><path d='M120 90 h70' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4'/><path d='M182 82 l14 8 l-14 8 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='232' y='58' width='20' height='64' rx='4' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='210' y='80' width='64' height='20' rx='4' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Open by asking learners what they do when they feel sick today - go to a clinic, take medicine, get a vaccine. Explain that people long ago also had ways to heal, and that medicine has changed a lot over time. This lesson looks at both traditional (older, handed-down) medicine and modern (science-based) medicine, and how they can work together.

slides.title: People have always tried to heal
slides.lead: For thousands of years, every community has looked for ways to fight illness and pain.

blocks.text: **Medicine** is the knowledge and skill of keeping people healthy and helping the sick get better. Long before hospitals and pills, people used **plants, prayers, rest and careful watching** to work out what helped a sick person recover.

items: Early healers learned by watching which plants made people feel better.
Knowledge was passed down from parents to children by word of mouth.
Over time, new discoveries changed medicine again and again.

slides.notes: Set up the whole unit's theme: continuity and change. People have ALWAYS wanted to heal (continuity), but the WAY they heal keeps changing (change). Ask learners to name a home remedy their family uses - this shows traditional knowledge is still alive today.

slides.title: Sangomas and inyangas
slides.lead: South Africa has a long tradition of indigenous healers who are still trusted by many people today.

blocks.heading: Isangoma
blocks.text: A spiritual healer, often a woman, who works out what is wrong by listening to the ancestors and looking at a person's whole life.

blocks.heading: Inyanga
blocks.text: A herbalist whose Zulu name means 'man of the trees'. Inyangas make medicine from plants, roots, bark and other natural things.

blocks.heading: Holistic care
blocks.text: Healers look at the body AND at worries at home or work, because they believe these can also make a person ill.

slides.notes: Treat this topic with respect - millions of South Africans use traditional healers, so this is living culture, not just history. Explain 'holistic': looking at the whole person, not only the sick part. Note that isangomas and inyangas often work in different ways but both aim to heal. Source: SAHO 'Indigenous medicine and traditional healing'.

slides.title: Healing plants and careful training
slides.lead: Traditional medicine is built on deep knowledge of the natural world.

blocks.text: An inyanga makes **muti** (medicine) from herbs, roots and bark. This knowledge is powerful and must be handled carefully - the **right plant in the right amount** can heal, but the wrong amount can be dangerous. That is why healers train for many years before they are allowed to treat people.

items: Healers say they are 'called' to the work by their ancestors.
Training includes strict rules, special diets and learning about many plants.
Some plants used long ago are still important in medicine today.

media.alt: Bundles of dried herbs, roots and a clay pot used to prepare traditional medicine.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><ellipse cx='110' cy='138' rx='70' ry='10' fill='#e6dcc7'/><path d='M60 130 q-14 -46 4 -70 q6 34 -4 70 Z' fill='#2f5233'/><path d='M60 130 q14 -46 -4 -70 q-6 34 4 70 Z' fill='#3f6b45'/><path d='M96 132 l10 -54 l10 54 Z' fill='#7a5a3a'/><path d='M104 96 l6 -6 l6 6' fill='none' stroke='#5c4327' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M150 132 q-8 -34 6 -52 q4 26 -6 52 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M150 118 a24 20 0 0 0 40 0 v6 a24 20 0 0 1 -40 0 Z' fill='#c07a3a'/><rect x='150' y='112' width='40' height='8' rx='3' fill='#a8632c'/></svg>

slides.notes: Emphasise the skill and responsibility involved - this counters the idea that traditional medicine is 'just superstition'. The link to slide 13 (Madagascar periwinkle used in modern cancer medicine) can be foreshadowed here: indigenous plant knowledge has helped modern science.

slides.title: Medicine in early societies
slides.lead: Across the world, ancient peoples slowly built up knowledge about the body and healing.

blocks.text: In places like **ancient Egypt**, healers set broken bones, treated wounds and wrote down their remedies. In many societies people mixed real cures with prayers and charms, because they did not yet know what actually caused illness.

items: People did not know that tiny **germs** cause many diseases.
Many believed illness was a punishment or came from bad air.
Even so, some early treatments really did work and were kept for centuries.

slides.notes: Keep this simple and comparative rather than a deep dive into any one civilisation. The key learning point is WHY early medicine was limited: people did not know germs existed. This sets up the huge change that comes with the discovery of germs and vaccines in later slides. [SME] Adjust the ancient-society example to match the exact civilisations named in your school's CAPS content.

slides.title: The smallpox epidemic
slides.lead: In 1713 a deadly disease reached the Cape and changed the lives of the people living there.

blocks.text: **Smallpox** arrived at the Cape in **1713**, brought by a passing Dutch ship. It spread quickly. The **Khoikhoi (Khoisan) people** were hit hardest of all, because the disease was new to them and their bodies had little protection against it.

items: An epidemic is when a disease spreads fast among many people.
There were no vaccines yet, so the disease could not be stopped.
Many Khoikhoi communities lost huge numbers of people.

media.alt: A sailing ship near the coast, with dotted lines showing disease spreading towards the land.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='108' width='240' height='52' fill='#a8bcae'/><path d='M0 108 q60 -18 120 0 t120 0 v52 H0 Z' fill='#c8b89a'/><g><rect x='40' y='88' width='40' height='16' fill='#7a5a3a'/><path d='M40 104 l6 10 h28 l6 -10 Z' fill='#5c4327'/><rect x='58' y='48' width='3' height='42' fill='#5c4327'/><path d='M61 52 h26 l-26 12 Z' fill='#f4efe6' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='1'/><path d='M61 68 h22 l-22 12 Z' fill='#f4efe6' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='1'/></g><g fill='#8a1f1f'><circle cx='110' cy='96' r='2.5'/><circle cx='130' cy='104' r='2.5'/><circle cx='150' cy='98' r='2.5'/><circle cx='170' cy='108' r='2.5'/><circle cx='190' cy='100' r='2.5'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the human cost gently but honestly: this epidemic caused great loss of life among the Khoikhoi and helped break up their communities. Introduce the vocabulary word 'epidemic'. Link forward: this is exactly the kind of disease that vaccines would later be able to stop. Source: SAHO 'Smallpox Epidemic Strikes at the Cape'.

slides.title: Three ideas that changed medicine
slides.lead: In the last 250 years, a few great discoveries made medicine far more powerful.

blocks.heading: Vaccines
blocks.text: In 1796 Edward Jenner showed that a mild germ could protect people from smallpox. This was the first vaccine.

blocks.heading: Germ theory
blocks.text: In the 1800s scientists like Louis Pasteur proved that tiny germs cause many diseases - so cleanliness could save lives.

blocks.heading: Antibiotics
blocks.text: In 1928 Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin, a medicine made from mould that kills harmful bacteria.

slides.notes: These three cards are the heart of 'key discoveries'. Stress the chain of cause and effect: once people understood germs, they understood why washing hands and clean water matter, and why vaccines and antibiotics work. Ask learners which of these they have benefited from (all three!). Dates are approximate turning points suitable for Grade 6.

slides.title: Clean water and public health
slides.lead: Once people understood germs, governments began working to stop diseases before they started.

blocks.text: **Public health** means caring for the health of a whole community, not just one sick person. When towns provided **clean water, drains and rubbish removal**, far fewer people caught diseases like cholera and typhoid that spread through dirty water.

items: Clean drinking water is one of the biggest life-savers in history.
Toilets and sewers keep germs away from where people live.
Vaccination programmes protect many children at once.

media.alt: A water tap with a clean drop of water and houses in the background, showing clean water reaching a town.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><g fill='#c8b89a'><rect x='24' y='70' width='34' height='50'/><rect x='64' y='60' width='34' height='60'/></g><path d='M24 70 l17 -16 l17 16 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M64 60 l17 -16 l17 16 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='150' y='40' width='10' height='44' fill='#6b7a8a'/><rect x='150' y='40' width='34' height='10' fill='#6b7a8a'/><rect x='150' y='84' width='24' height='8' fill='#8a97a5'/><path d='M162 96 q-6 10 0 16 q6 -6 0 -16 Z' fill='#4a86c4'/><rect x='24' y='120' width='160' height='6' fill='#7a8b5a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Public health is a key CAPS idea: change came not only from clever cures but from prevention. Make it concrete - ask what would happen if the school had no clean water or toilets. Connect back to germ theory (slide 7): we clean because we now know germs spread disease.

slides.title: The 'Black October' flu
slides.lead: In 1918 a fast-spreading flu swept across South Africa and the world.

blocks.text: In **September and October 1918**, the **Spanish Flu** reached South Africa, first appearing at the ports. It spread so fast that October 1918 became known as **'Black October'**. Because there were no antibiotics yet, doctors could not cure the pneumonia that killed many patients.

items: Around 300,000 people are thought to have died in South Africa.
Mineworkers and poorer communities were among the worst affected.
Schools, shops and public places had to close to slow the spread.

media.alt: A calendar page turned to October with a face mask beside it, marking the 1918 flu.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='30' y='34' width='92' height='96' rx='6' fill='#fff' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='30' y='34' width='92' height='22' rx='6' fill='#8a1f1f'/><g fill='#8a1f1f'><rect x='42' y='66' width='12' height='10'/><rect x='60' y='66' width='12' height='10'/><rect x='78' y='66' width='12' height='10'/><rect x='96' y='66' width='12' height='10'/><rect x='42' y='82' width='12' height='10'/><rect x='60' y='82' width='12' height='10'/><rect x='78' y='82' width='12' height='10'/></g><path d='M150 70 q22 -12 44 0 v20 q-22 12 -44 0 Z' fill='#dcd3c2' stroke='#999' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='150' y1='74' x2='140' y2='66' stroke='#999' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='194' y1='74' x2='204' y2='66' stroke='#999' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='150' y1='86' x2='140' y2='94' stroke='#999' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='194' y1='86' x2='204' y2='94' stroke='#999' stroke-width='1.5'/></svg>

slides.notes: Learners often connect this to recent pandemics, which helps them understand it. Explain that closing schools and shops to slow a disease is a public-health tool still used today. [SME] Death-toll estimates for the 1918 flu in South Africa vary widely (roughly 250,000-350,000); confirm the exact figure your SAHO source and CAPS material use. Sources: SAHO 'The Influenza epidemic' and 'Spanish Flu strikes South Africa'.

slides.title: When health rules were not fair
slides.lead: Sometimes fear of disease was used in unfair ways.

blocks.text: In **1901**, cases of **bubonic plague** were found in Cape Town. The government used the fear of plague as a reason to **force many black residents out of the city** and move them to a separate area. Health worries were real, but the way people were treated was **unfair and unequal**.

items: Everyone deserves fair and equal health care.
Illness was sometimes blamed on the poorest people instead of poor living conditions.
Studying this helps us see why fair health care matters today.

slides.notes: Handle this sensitively and at an age-appropriate level. The point for Grade 6 is a values lesson about fairness, not a detailed study of segregation. This event (the removals to Uitvlugt/Ndabeni) was an early step towards later forced separation. Keep the tone factual and hopeful: today we aim for health care that treats everyone equally. [SME] Please review the wording for age-appropriateness and accuracy. Source: SAHO 'Three cases of bubonic plague are reported in Cape Town'.

slides.title: The world's first heart transplant
slides.lead: On 3 December 1967 a South African team did something no one had ever done before.

blocks.text: At **Groote Schuur Hospital** in Cape Town, **Dr Christiaan Barnard** and his team performed the **world's first human heart transplant**. They gave a new heart to a very sick patient, **Louis Washkansky**. It was a huge moment that made doctors around the world believe transplants were possible.

items: The operation lasted about nine hours and used the skills of a large team.
Hamilton Naki, a self-taught assistant, helped develop the surgery skills behind it.
The patient lived for 18 days before dying of pneumonia - but medicine had changed forever.

media.alt: A stylised heart with a plus sign, showing a medical breakthrough.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M100 132 C40 92 44 44 74 44 c14 0 22 10 26 18 c4 -8 12 -18 26 -18 c30 0 34 48 -26 88 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='150' y='40' width='10' height='34' rx='2' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='138' y='52' width='34' height='10' rx='2' fill='#2f5233'/><path d='M64 96 l12 0 l6 -12 l8 24 l6 -12 l12 0' fill='none' stroke='#fff' stroke-width='3'/></svg>

slides.notes: A proud South African achievement and a chance to celebrate a local story. Importantly, name Hamilton Naki: a black South African whose surgical skill was under-recognised for decades because of apartheid - a fair, honest addition. Keep the fact that Washkansky died after 18 days: it shows early breakthroughs are just the beginning, and that later transplants became far more successful. Sources: SAHO dated events on Barnard and Naki.

slides.title: Medicine through time

blocks.alt: A timeline of medical events from 1713 to 1967.

events.label: Traditional healers use plants and holistic care
Plague scare used to move people in Cape Town
Spanish Flu 'Black October' in South Africa
World's first heart transplant, Cape Town

slides.notes: Use this to consolidate the sequence and the theme of change over time. Ask learners to spot the pattern: the earliest problems (like smallpox) could not be stopped, but later discoveries (vaccines, antibiotics, surgery) gave people much more power over disease. Traditional healing runs alongside the whole timeline, not just at the start.

slides.title: Traditional and modern medicine together
slides.lead: In South Africa today, both kinds of medicine are still important.

blocks.text: Many South Africans visit **both** a clinic and a traditional healer. Scientists have even found that some plants used by traditional healers really do work - for example, the **Madagascar periwinkle**, long used in traditional medicine, is now used in **modern cancer treatment**.

items: Traditional knowledge can help scientists find new medicines.
Modern medicine offers vaccines, surgery and antibiotics.
Respecting both helps care for the whole community.

slides.notes: End the content on a balanced, respectful note that reflects real South African life. This avoids the false idea that modern medicine simply 'replaced' traditional medicine. The periwinkle example is a memorable, true link between the two worlds. Source: SAHO 'Indigenous medicine and traditional healing'.

slides.title: What we learned
slides.lead: Medicine has changed enormously over time, but the wish to heal has always stayed the same.

blocks.text: We saw **traditional healers** and healing plants, deadly **epidemics** like smallpox and the 1918 flu, great **discoveries** such as vaccines and antibiotics, the importance of **public health**, and South Africa's own **first heart transplant**. Now show what you remember.

blocks.question: Name the two main types of traditional healers in South Africa and say what each one does.
Choose ONE key discovery (vaccines, germ theory or antibiotics) and explain why it helped people.
Why is clean water so important for keeping a whole community healthy?

slides.notes: Use the three questions as a written task or class discussion. Question 1 tests recall (sangoma/inyanga), Question 2 tests understanding of a key discovery, and Question 3 links to public health. Mark allocations follow a simple Intermediate Phase style. Close by reminding learners that both traditional and modern medicine matter in South Africa today.
